evals.name: landing-page-bold-aesthetic
evals.prompt: I need a landing page for my new SaaS product called 'Meridian' — it's a project management tool for creative agencies. The vibe should feel premium and editorial, not the usual tech startup look. Dark theme, sophisticated typography, some subtle animations on scroll. React + Tailwind project, dev server runs on localhost:3000.
evals.expected_output: Generates a distinctive landing page with editorial aesthetic, non-generic font choices, dark theme with rich backgrounds, scroll-triggered animations, and attempts to verify it visually in the browser.

assertions.text: Does not use Inter, Roboto, Arial, or system-ui as the primary display font — chooses a distinctive alternative
Uses rich dark backgrounds (not pure #000000) with tinted or warm undertones
Defines a color system using CSS custom properties or Tailwind theme extension for consistency
Includes scroll-triggered or staggered entrance animations with CSS or a motion library
Includes a prefers-reduced-motion media query or check to disable animations for accessibility
Attempts to open the result in a browser (Chrome extension, Playwright MCP, or mentions the need to verify visually)
The overall aesthetic direction is clearly editorial/premium — not a generic tech startup template

evals.name: dashboard-with-verification-loop
evals.prompt: Build me an analytics dashboard for our internal metrics tool. It should have a sidebar nav, a main content area with some charts (use placeholder data), and a header with user avatar. Make it look really polished — I'm tired of dashboards that all look the same. I have the Chrome extension connected, so you can check how it looks.
evals.expected_output: Generates a visually distinctive dashboard, then uses the Chrome extension to navigate to it, visually verify the layout, check for console errors, and iterate if anything looks off.

assertions.text: Implements a sidebar navigation + main content area layout structure
Makes deliberate aesthetic choices that differentiate it from a generic dashboard template (unique colors, typography, or spatial composition)
Uses Chrome extension MCP tools (or describes using them) to navigate to the running app and visually inspect the result
Checks the browser console for errors or warnings during verification
If visual issues are found during browser verification, makes code changes and re-verifies (or describes this workflow)
Uses realistic placeholder data for charts and metrics — not empty states or lorem ipsum

evals.name: playwright-mcp-fallback
evals.prompt: I need a pricing page for our B2B product. Three tiers, toggle between monthly/annual, feature comparison table at the bottom. The Playwright MCP server is set up but I don't have the Chrome extension. Make sure it actually looks good — take screenshots to verify. Next.js project on port 3000.
evals.expected_output: Generates a pricing page, then uses Playwright MCP to navigate, take screenshots, check the accessibility tree, and verify the visual result. Falls back to Playwright MCP since Chrome extension is unavailable.

assertions.text: Implements three distinct pricing tiers with clear visual hierarchy (one tier emphasized as recommended/popular)
Includes a functional toggle between monthly and annual pricing with price updates
Uses Playwright MCP browser_take_screenshot (or equivalent) to capture and verify the rendered page
Uses Playwright MCP browser_navigate to open the dev server URL
Uses browser_snapshot or similar to check the accessibility tree structure, or mentions accessibility compliance
Avoids the generic purple-gradient-on-white pricing page pattern

evals.name: no-browser-tools-graceful-fallback
evals.prompt: Create a contact form page for my portfolio site. Clean, minimal design with a subtle background texture. Plain HTML/CSS/JS, no framework. I don't have any browser extensions or MCP servers set up.
evals.expected_output: Generates a polished contact form with minimal aesthetic, uses vanilla HTML/CSS/JS, and since no browser tools are available, suggests setting up Chrome extension or Playwright MCP for visual verification.

assertions.text: Uses plain HTML/CSS/JS without any framework dependency
Implements a clean, minimal design direction as requested — not overloaded with effects
Includes a subtle background texture (noise, grain, gradient mesh, or similar)
Mentions or recommends setting up Chrome extension or Playwright MCP for visual verification since no browser tools are available
The contact form has proper form elements (input, textarea, button) with appropriate types and labels
Includes visible focus states on form inputs for keyboard accessibility

evals.name: responsive-verification
evals.prompt: I've got this hero section but it looks broken on mobile. Here's the component at src/components/Hero.tsx — can you fix the responsive layout and verify it works at different screen sizes? Playwright MCP is available.
evals.expected_output: Reads the existing component, fixes responsive issues, then uses Playwright MCP to verify at multiple viewport sizes (mobile, tablet, desktop) by resizing and taking screenshots.

assertions.text: Reads the existing Hero.tsx file before making changes (doesn't rewrite from scratch without understanding current code)
Addresses responsive layout issues using media queries, flexbox/grid adjustments, or responsive utility classes
Verifies the result at multiple viewport widths (at least mobile and desktop) using browser_resize or similar
Takes screenshots at different viewport sizes to confirm the responsive fix works
Preserves the original design intent while fixing the responsive issues — doesn't redesign the component

evals.name: console-error-detection-and-fix
evals.prompt: I just built this product card grid but something's not right — the images aren't loading and I think there might be JS errors. Can you check it in the browser and fix whatever's wrong? Chrome extension is connected, app running on localhost:5173.
evals.expected_output: Uses Chrome extension to navigate to the page, identifies console errors and broken images, fixes the underlying code issues, and re-verifies in the browser.

assertions.text: Navigates to localhost:5173 using available browser tools to inspect the running application
Checks the browser console for JavaScript errors or warnings
Identifies specific issues (broken image URLs, JS errors, or similar) from the browser inspection
Makes code changes to fix the identified issues
Returns to the browser after fixing to confirm the issues are resolved
